children.name: Sarcophilus
children.description: The Tasmanian devil — the world's largest carnivorous marsupial, and one of the most charismatic endangered animals on Earth. Devils are powerfully built scavengers and hunters with the strongest bite force relative to body size of any living mammal, capable of consuming every part of a carcass including dense bones. They are intensely social scavengers, communicating their hierarchical status with blood-curdling screams and communal feeding rituals. Mainland devils were driven extinct around 3,000 years ago following the introduction of dingoes; they survived on Tasmania where dingoes never arrived. Since 1996, devil facial tumour disease — one of only three known transmissible cancers — has killed over 80% of the Tasmanian population. Conservation breeding programs and reintroductions to mainland Australia (where dingoes have been excluded) offer hope.

children.name: Sarcophilus harrisii
children.description: Endangered. Population reduced from approximately 140,000 to fewer than 25,000 by devil facial tumour disease since 1996. Reintroduced to Barrington Tops, NSW in 2020 — the first wild devils on mainland Australia in 3,000 years.

children.name: Dasyurus
children.description: Quolls — spotted, cat-like marsupial carnivores of Australia and New Guinea. Six species range from mouse-sized to cat-sized, filling ecological niches comparable to small wildcats. The spotted-tailed quoll is Australia's largest surviving native carnivore on the mainland. All four mainland quoll species have declined severely since European settlement, driven by the introduction of foxes, cats, and cane toads, and the collapse of native prey populations. Quolls are energetic hunters of mammals, birds, reptiles, and insects, and their loss has cascading effects on prey populations.

children.name: Dasyurus viverrinus
children.description: Extinct on the Australian mainland since the 1960s. Survives in Tasmania and in fenced predator-free reserves on the mainland where it is being reintroduced. Exists in two colour morphs — fawn and black.

children.name: Dasyurus maculatus
children.description: Australia's largest native mammalian carnivore. Endangered on the mainland. Unique among quolls in having spots on its tail as well as its body. An agile climber that hunts possums and gliders in the canopy.

children.name: Dasyurus hallucatus
children.description: Endangered and the smallest of the Australian quolls. Restricted to rocky outcrops and savanna woodlands across northern Australia. Devastated by the spread of cane toads, which are fatally toxic when eaten; conservation programs include training quolls to avoid toads.

children.name: Dasyurus geoffroii
children.description: Once ranged across 70% of Australia; now confined to the south-west corner of Western Australia following fox and cat predation. A nocturnal, solitary hunter of birds, reptiles, and small mammals in jarrah and wandoo woodland.

children.name: Dasyurus albopunctatus
children.description: Endemic to the mountains of New Guinea above 1,000 m. A medium-sized spotted quoll of montane rainforest and grassland, hunting insects, small vertebrates, and carrion. Data deficient; little studied in the wild.

children.name: Dasyurus spartacus
children.description: Restricted to the Trans-Fly savanna of southern New Guinea. Distinguished from other New Guinea quolls by its bronze-brown dorsal colouration and relatively large body size. Vulnerable due to habitat loss and hunting.

children.name: Antechinus
children.description: Antechinuses — small, mouse-like marsupials of Australian forest, famous for one of the most extreme reproductive strategies of any mammal. In every species, all males die after a frenzied two-week mating season. Males suppress their immune system, stop producing blood cells, and experience the equivalent of a catastrophic cortisol-driven stress collapse — their fur falls out, they bleed internally, and they continue mating even as they are literally dying. All males are dead before the young are born. This 'suicidal reproduction' is thought to be an adaptation to a narrow window of insect abundance — with no males surviving to compete for food, the females and young have exclusive access to the peak resource.

children.name: Antechinus stuartii
children.description: A forest antechinus of eastern Australia. All males die within three weeks of the synchronized annual mating season — one of the most dramatic examples of semelparous reproduction (die-after-mating) in any vertebrate.

children.name: Antechinus agilis
children.description: Formerly lumped with A. stuartii and separated on molecular grounds in 1998. Occupies wet sclerophyll forests and rainforest margins in south-eastern Australia. Slightly smaller and greyer than the brown antechinus, and partially separated from it geographically.

children.name: Antechinus swainsonii
children.description: The largest of the antechinuses, dark brown to black in colour. Inhabits cool, wet forests and subalpine zones of south-eastern Australia and Tasmania, where it forages under logs, rocks, and leaf litter for invertebrates and small vertebrates.

children.name: Antechinus flavipes
children.description: The most widespread antechinus, spanning coastal forests from Queensland to Western Australia. Distinguished by its rusty-yellow flanks and feet contrasting with a grey-brown back. Occupies a broader range of habitats than most congeners.

children.name: Antechinus minimus
children.description: A small, boldly marked antechinus of coastal heathland, sedge, and tussock grass in south-eastern Australia and Flinders Island. Ground-dwelling and largely diurnal; forages through low vegetation for insects and spiders.

children.name: Antechinus adustus
children.description: Restricted to the wet tropical rainforests of north-eastern Queensland, including the Atherton Tablelands. Rusty-red in colouration on the sides. Described as a distinct species in 2014 following genetic analysis.

children.name: Antechinus godmani
children.description: A rainforest antechinus endemic to the Wet Tropics of north-eastern Queensland, occurring above 600 m altitude. Identified as a distinct species in 2014; named after Frederick Du Cane Godman, a 19th-century zoologist.

children.name: Antechinus leo
children.description: Named for the lion-like cinnamon colour of its flanks, this species is endemic to Cape York Peninsula rainforests in northern Queensland. It forages at low heights in the forest understorey and is near threatened due to habitat loss.

children.name: Antechinus billardierii
children.description: Endemic to Tasmania and its offshore islands. A robust, dark antechinus of rainforest and wet eucalypt forest. Behaviourally similar to mainland congeners but benefits from the absence of foxes and significant predator pressure on Tasmania.

children.name: Antechinus argentus
children.description: Described as new to science in 2014, endemic to the Kroombit Tops and Gamboin National Parks in central Queensland. Distinguished by a pale silvery head contrasting with a darker body. Critically endangered due to its extremely restricted range.

children.name: Antechinus arktos
children.description: Discovered in 2014 on Springbrook Plateau in south-east Queensland. One of the most recently described antechinus species, already classified as endangered due to its tiny range and vulnerability to climate change reducing its cool, moist montane habitat.

children.name: Antechinus mysticus
children.description: Named for its long cryptic status, described in 2016 after molecular work separated it from A. subtropicus. Inhabits subtropical rainforest and wet sclerophyll at mid-elevations in south-east Queensland and northern New South Wales.

children.name: Antechinus subtropicus
children.description: Described in 1998, occupying subtropical rainforest and wet sclerophyll of the Border Ranges and Lamington Plateau on the Queensland-New South Wales border. Similar in appearance to A. stuartii but inhabiting higher, cooler zones.

children.name: Antechinus mimetes
children.description: Separated from A. swainsonii in 2015 on the basis of genetic and morphological evidence. Occupies cool montane forests of south-eastern mainland Australia. Darker and slightly smaller than the Tasmanian dusky antechinus.

children.name: Antechinus vandycki
children.description: Described in 2022, known from the Pilliga Scrub in central New South Wales — the largest remaining area of inland dry sclerophyll forest in eastern Australia. Named after mammalogist Peter van Dyck.

children.name: Sminthopsis
children.description: Dunnarts — small, mouse-like dasyurid marsupials of Australia and New Guinea, some of the smallest marsupials. Dunnarts are fierce insectivores and small vertebrate predators, with pointed snouts and large eyes. Like many small dasyurids, they store fat in their tails during periods of abundance and can enter torpor during cold nights or food shortage. The fat-tailed dunnart is a model organism in marsupial research. Several species have declined severely due to changes in fire regimes, land clearing, and introduced predators.

children.name: Sminthopsis crassicaudata
children.description: A model species in marsupial reproductive biology. Stores fat in a swollen, carrot-shaped tail as an energy reserve. Can enter daily torpor to conserve energy. Common in southern Australia's open habitats.

children.name: Sminthopsis murina
children.description: One of the most widespread dunnarts, occurring across southern and eastern Australia in heath, scrub, and open woodland. Grey-brown above with a white belly; distinguished from related species by the lack of a strongly fattened tail.

children.name: Sminthopsis leucopus
children.description: A forest dunnart of south-eastern Australia and Tasmania, occupying coastal heath, dry sclerophyll forest, and heathy woodland. Named for its white feet; nocturnal and largely solitary, hunting invertebrates and small lizards.

children.name: Sminthopsis macroura
children.description: Characterised by a dark brown stripe running from snout to forehead between large, prominent eyes. An arid-zone species across central Australia, sheltering in soil cracks and burrows during the heat of the day and hunting insects and small reptiles at night.

children.name: Sminthopsis lanigera
children.description: Distinctive for its woolly, pencil-shaped tail. Endemic to south-western Western Australia, where it inhabits mallee scrub, heath, and open woodland. One of the least-studied Australian dunnarts; rarely trapped.

children.name: Sminthopsis longicaudata
children.description: Has the longest tail relative to body size of any dunnart — often more than twice the head-body length. Restricted to rocky outcrops in the arid interior of Western Australia. Rarely observed; known mainly from specimen records and incidental trapping.

children.name: Sminthopsis douglasi
children.description: Endangered. One of the largest dunnarts, known only from the Mitchell grass plains around Julia Creek in north-western Queensland. The dense, tussocky grass habitat is threatened by overgrazing and altered fire regimes.

children.name: Sminthopsis aitkeni
children.description: Critically endangered and restricted to remnant heath on Kangaroo Island, South Australia. One of the most threatened marsupials in Australia; the 2019-20 bushfires burned much of its remaining habitat. Named after naturalist Jack Aitken.

children.name: Sminthopsis psammophila
children.description: Critically endangered. Known from fewer than five isolated populations on the Eyre Peninsula, Yellabinna, and Yalata in South Australia. Inhabits dense, spinifex-covered sand dunes and mallee scrub. Cat predation is the primary threat.

children.name: Sminthopsis dolichura
children.description: A small dunnart of heath, mallee, and dry sclerophyll forest in southern Western Australia. Slender with a moderately elongated tail. Little studied but appears to require dense low shrub cover for shelter and foraging.

children.name: Sminthopsis fuliginosus
children.description: Dark sooty-grey in colour, matching the bark and granite outcrops of its south-western Western Australian range. Inhabits jarrah and marri woodland, heath, and mallee. Feeds on invertebrates and spiders at night.

children.name: Sminthopsis ooldea
children.description: Named after Ooldea on the Nullarbor Plain where specimens were first collected. Inhabits spinifex grassland and shrubby mallee of the arid interior. Very similar to S. crassicaudata but occurring in drier, more arid habitats.

children.name: Sminthopsis hirtipes
children.description: Specialised for life on loose sand dunes: the soles of its feet are covered in stiff bristle-like hairs that spread its weight and prevent sinking. Inhabits spinifex-covered desert dunes across central and western Australia.

children.name: Sminthopsis youngsoni
children.description: Smaller and paler than S. hirtipes, with similar hairy-foot adaptations for sand dune locomotion. Occurs in spinifex sandplains and dunes across arid Australia. Named after mammalogist Andrew Youngson.

children.name: Sminthopsis granulipes
children.description: Distinctive for its white, club-shaped, finely granular tail tip — unique among dunnarts. Found in mallee and heath of the southern coast of Western Australia. Poorly known; seldom captured in routine pitfall surveys.

children.name: Sminthopsis gilberti
children.description: Named after the naturalist and collector John Gilbert, who worked in the south-west of Australia in the 1840s. Occupies heath, mallee, and dry woodland in the south-west; distinguished from S. murina by sharper facial markings.

children.name: Sminthopsis griseoventer
children.description: Named for its grey (rather than white) underparts. Found in heath, mallee, and dry sclerophyll forest along the coast and ranges of southern Western Australia and South Australia. Moderately common in undisturbed habitat.

children.name: Sminthopsis callipygus
children.description: Restricted to the Pilbara region of north-western Western Australia, where it inhabits rocky slopes and spinifex grassland. A robust dunnart with a thick-based tail. Described in 1975; among the less-studied members of the genus.

children.name: Sminthopsis butleri
children.description: Known from a handful of specimens from the Kimberley region of north-western Australia and possibly adjacent New Guinea. Named after William Harry Butler, a prominent Australian naturalist and conservationist.

children.name: Sminthopsis virginiae
children.description: The most brightly coloured dunnart, with vivid russet-red cheeks and flanks. Found in coastal northern Australia and southern New Guinea in tall grass, monsoon forest edges, and paperbark wetland margins.

children.name: Sminthopsis archeri
children.description: Found only in the Cape York Peninsula and the Trans-Fly region of New Guinea. Chestnut-orange on the dorsum with a pale belly. Occurs in open eucalypt woodland and seasonal wetland margins. Named after taxonomist W. A. Archer.

children.name: Sminthopsis bindi
children.description: Described in 2020 from specimens long assigned to S. virginiae; its name comes from the Bininj/Mungguy word for dunnart. Restricted to the rocky escarpment and monsoon forest patches of the Kakadu region of the Northern Territory.

children.name: Ningaui
children.description: Ningauis — tiny, fierce predators of arid and semi-arid Australia. Among the smallest marsupials, weighing only 2–9 g, ningauis punch well above their weight: they routinely kill prey larger than themselves, including large insects, spiders, and small lizards. The name derives from an Aboriginal Australian spirit creature. Three species occupy spinifex grassland, mulga shrubland, and heath across the continent.

children.name: Ningaui timealeyi
children.description: Restricted to spinifex grassland in the Pilbara region of north-western Western Australia. One of the world's smallest marsupials at under 9 g. Hunts large invertebrates at night among spinifex clumps.

children.name: Ningaui ridei
children.description: The most widespread ningaui, occurring across the arid interior in spinifex and mulga shrubland. Named after Australian mammalogist W. D. L. Ride. Females carry up to six young in the pouch. Can enter torpor during cold desert nights.

children.name: Ningaui yvonneae
children.description: Found in heath, mallee, and Triodia grassland across southern Australia. The largest of the three ningaui species. Named after Yvonne Muirhead, who assisted in its collection.

children.name: Planigale
children.description: Planigales — the flattest-headed marsupials. Their skulls are laterally compressed to allow them to pursue prey into narrow rock crevices and soil cracks — a feature so extreme that the skull of the common planigale is less than 3 mm deep. Despite weighing as little as 4 g, planigales are voracious predators of invertebrates and small lizards. Five species span Australia and New Guinea, from tropical savanna to semi-arid grassland.

children.name: Planigale maculata
children.description: The most commonly encountered planigale, occurring across coastal northern and eastern Australia in grassland, cane fields, and open woodland. Produces the largest litters of any marsupial — up to 12 young. Active year-round.

children.name: Planigale ingrami
children.description: One of the world's smallest marsupials — adults weigh as little as 4.3 g. Inhabits cracking clay soils and Mitchell grass plains of northern Australia. Named after taxonomist Sir William Ingram.

children.name: Planigale tenuirostris
children.description: Restricted to cracking clay soils and black-soil plains of inland New South Wales and Queensland. The narrowest-snouted planigale; uses its pointed snout to probe soil cracks for invertebrates. Population status poorly known.

children.name: Planigale gilesi
children.description: Named for its reduced (paucident) lower premolars — a useful diagnostic character. Found in semi-arid mulga, grassland, and saltbush plains of central and western Australia. Named after explorer Ernest Giles.

children.name: Planigale novaeguineae
children.description: The only planigale occurring in New Guinea, found in savanna and grassland habitats in the Trans-Fly lowlands. Very small and poorly known; biology inferred largely from its Australian relatives.

children.name: Dasykaluta
children.description: A monotypic genus erected for the red-eared antechinus of the Pilbara. Dasykaluta superficially resembles an antechinus but belongs to a distinct lineage. Like the antechinuses, males are semelparous — all die after a brief annual mating season. The genus name combines Dasy- (hairy) with Kaluta, the local Aboriginal name.

children.name: Dasykaluta rosamondae
children.description: A small, vividly rust-red marsupial of the Pilbara region of north-western Western Australia. Males exhibit the same suicidal mating behaviour as true antechinuses; all die after a brief annual rut in August. Named after Rosamond, the wife of the describing zoologist.

children.name: Parantechinus
children.description: Parantechinuses — two species of medium-sized dasyurids from arid and semi-arid Australia. Unlike the true antechinuses, parantechinus males are not strictly semelparous: some males survive their first breeding season. Both species are rarely encountered; the dibbler is one of the most critically threatened Australian mammals.

children.name: Parantechinus apicalis
children.description: Critically endangered. Believed extinct until rediscovered in 1967 on the south coast of Western Australia and on offshore islands. Distinguished by a distinctive white eye-ring and speckled brown-grey coat. Inhabits coastal heath and mallee scrub.

children.name: Parantechinus bilarni
children.description: Found in the rocky sandstone ranges of the Northern Territory and Kimberley, where it shelters in rock crevices and hunts invertebrates at night. Less threatened than its southern relative but rarely observed.

children.name: Pseudantechinus
children.description: Pseudantechinuses — six species of small, fat-tailed dasyurids of arid and rocky Australia. They store fat in the tail and can use torpor to survive lean periods. Despite resembling antechinuses, they are not closely related; males typically survive beyond the first breeding season. Several species are restricted to narrow rocky habitat and are poorly known.

children.name: Pseudantechinus macdonnellensis
children.description: The most widespread and best-known pseudantechinus, found on rocky ranges across central Australia including the MacDonnell Ranges, Pilbara, and Kimberley. Stores fat in a carrot-shaped tail. Males may survive more than one breeding season.

children.name: Pseudantechinus ningbing
children.description: Named after the Ningbing Range in the Kimberley region of Western Australia, where it was first collected. Inhabits rocky sandstone outcrops in the north Kimberley. Rarely encountered; biology largely unknown.

children.name: Pseudantechinus roryi
children.description: Described in 2000 from the east Kimberley and Victoria River district of the Northern Territory. Occupies rocky terrain in mulga and spinifex country. Named after zoologist Rory Cooper.

children.name: Pseudantechinus mimulus
children.description: Restricted to rocky outcrops and gorges in the Gulf of Carpentaria region and nearby ranges. Smallest of the pseudantechinuses. Very little field data; known mostly from museum specimens.

children.name: Pseudantechinus woolleyae
children.description: Found on rocky ranges near Shark Bay and the Murchison River in Western Australia. Named after marsupial biologist Patricia Woolley, a leading expert on dasyurids.

children.name: Pseudantechinus bilarni
children.description: Described in 1948; one of the first pseudantechinuses named. From rocky areas of the Barkly Tablelands and surrounds in the Northern Territory. Bilarni refers to an Aboriginal name for the region.

children.name: Dasycercus
children.description: Mulgaras — two species of mid-sized, thick-tailed dasyurids of the arid interior. One of the dominant small predators of Australian desert, mulgaras subsist entirely on live prey — insects, spiders, lizards, and small rodents — and obtain all their water from their food. Their fat-storing tail swells markedly when food is plentiful.

children.name: Dasycercus cristicauda
children.description: Named for the crest of black hairs on the upper tail surface. Inhabits sandy spinifex and grassland across the arid interior. Digs extensive burrow systems in sand dunes. Vulnerable due to altered fire regimes and predation.

children.name: Dasycercus blythi
children.description: Distinguished from the crest-tailed mulgara by a brush of black hairs covering the entire distal tail. Occupies stony gibber plains and clay-loam grassland in central Australia. Separated from D. cristicauda as a distinct species in 2004.

children.name: Myoictis
children.description: Three-striped dasyures of New Guinea — medium-sized forest dasyurids with distinctive dorsal striping. Myoictis species occupy primary and secondary rainforest and monsoon forest from sea level to montane zones across New Guinea and some adjacent islands. They are solitary, nocturnal predators of large invertebrates and small vertebrates.

children.name: Myoictis melas
children.description: One of the most frequently encountered New Guinea dasyurids. Has three dark dorsal stripes running from shoulder to rump on a brown background. Found in primary and secondary forest across lowland and lower montane New Guinea.

children.name: Myoictis leucura
children.description: Characterised by a long white tail contrasting with its brown striped body. Recorded from scattered lowland localities across southern New Guinea. Rarely collected; status and ecology poorly understood.

children.name: Myoictis wallacii
children.description: Named after Alfred Russel Wallace, who collected natural history specimens across the Malay Archipelago in the 19th century. Known from a few islands west of New Guinea. Poorly known.

children.name: Myoictis wavicus
children.description: Described in 2010 from specimens collected in the Wavi area of the Mamberamo River basin in Papua. The most recently recognised Myoictis species; separated from M. melas on the basis of morphological and molecular data.

children.name: Murexia
children.description: Short-furred dasyures of New Guinea — forest-dwelling dasyurids known from New Guinea and the Aru Islands. Murexia species are small to medium-sized nocturnal predators of the rainforest floor and understorey. The genus has been subject to repeated revision; species formerly placed in Phascolosorex and Antechinus have been moved here.

children.name: Murexia longicaudata
children.description: Found in rainforest and forest edge habitats across much of New Guinea up to 2,300 m. Named for its long tail, which exceeds head-body length. Hunts invertebrates and small vertebrates on the forest floor and in low vegetation.

children.name: Murexia rothschildi
children.description: A larger New Guinea dasyure restricted to montane forest above 1,500 m on the main cordillera. Named after Walter Rothschild. Rarely collected; considered data deficient.

children.name: Phascolosorex
children.description: Marsupial shrews of New Guinea — two species of slender, shrew-like dasyurids restricted to the mountains of New Guinea above 1,000 m. The dorsal striping of Phascolosorex dorsalis makes it one of the most visually distinctive New Guinea dasyurids. Little is known of the ecology of either species.

children.name: Phascolosorex dorsalis
children.description: Has a prominent dark dorsal stripe from nape to tail base. Inhabits montane rainforest and grassland above 1,500 m in the central ranges of New Guinea. Shrew-like in appearance; hunts invertebrates in dense vegetation.

children.name: Phascolosorex doriae
children.description: Distinguished by a rufous-red ventral surface. Known from montane forests above 1,200 m in the Vogelkop and central ranges of New Guinea. Named after Italian naturalist Giacomo Doria.

children.name: Neophascogale
children.description: A monotypic genus containing only the speckled dasyure of New Guinea's central mountains. Neophascogale lorentzii is one of the larger New Guinea dasyurids, distinguished by long, coarse fur and pale speckled patterning. It occupies montane forest above 1,500 m and is considered near threatened.

children.name: Neophascogale lorentzii
children.description: A large, shaggy-furred dasyurid of New Guinea's central mountain ranges above 1,500 m. Speckled white on brown dorsal fur gives a frosted appearance. A solitary hunter of invertebrates and small vertebrates in moss forest.

children.name: Micromurexia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Habbema dasyure, a very small, short-furred forest dasyurid from the highlands of West Papua. Separated from Murexia on molecular and morphological grounds; rarely collected and poorly known.

children.name: Micromurexia habbema
children.description: Known only from the Lake Habbema area of the Star Mountains in Papua, Indonesia, at elevations around 3,000 m. A tiny, mouse-sized dasyurid of high-altitude moss forest. Among the least-studied marsupials in the world.

children.name: Phascogale
children.description: Phascogales — two species of tree-climbing, pouched dasyurids of Australian forest. Agile and acrobatic, they forage for invertebrates and small vertebrates in the bark and hollows of large eucalypts. Like the antechinuses, males are semelparous — all die after the annual mating season. The brush-tailed phascogale has one of the most spectacular tails of any marsupial: the distal third is covered in long, bushy black hairs that flare out when the animal is alarmed.

children.name: Phascogale tapoatafa
children.description: A sleek, silver-grey marsupial with a spectacular bushy black tail tip. Occupies dry sclerophyll forest and woodland across southern and south-western Australia. Males die after the brief August mating season; females may live three years.

children.name: Paramurexia
children.description: A monotypic genus for the Broad-striped dasyure of New Guinea — a medium-sized, boldly patterned forest dasyurid with a wide dark dorsal stripe. Sometimes placed in Murexia; molecular work supports its separation. Found in lowland and hill forest of Papua New Guinea.

children.name: Paramurexia rothschildi
children.description: Has a striking broad dark stripe along its entire dorsum. Found in lowland and foothill rainforest of Papua New Guinea and the Aru Islands. Rarely recorded since its original description; conservation status poorly assessed.
